cards.title: Microbiology — Common Pathogens & Infections

alerts.label: Microbiology in Clinical Practice
alerts.text: Microbiology guides antibiotic selection, infection control, and understanding of pathogenesis. BNYS practitioners must recognise when infection requires immediate referral, understand culture reports, and know which herbs/supplements have evidence-based antimicrobial or immunomodulatory activity that can complement or bridge conventional treatment.

rows: Community Pneumonia (CAP)
S. pneumoniae ★, H. influenzae, Mycoplasma (atypical), Klebsiella (India — alcohol/DM)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate + azithromycin. Severe: ceftriaxone + azithromycin
Vit D 50,000IU single dose (deficiency = ↑ CAP risk). Elderberry (antiviral if viral CAP). NAC 600mg BD (mucolytic). Steam inhalation + postural drainage.
UTI (uncomplicated)
E. coli ★ (80%), Klebsiella, Staphylococcus saprophyticus (young women)
Nitrofurantoin 100mg BD × 5 days (India: resistance to cotrimoxazole high). Fosfomycin 3g single dose.
D-mannose 2g BD (prevents E. coli adhesion ★★★). Cranberry extract (PAC content — prophylaxis). Uva ursi 250mg TID (antiseptic). Hydration 2L/day. Probiotics (Lactobacillus reuteri RC-14).
Skin & Soft Tissue Infection (SSTI)
S. aureus ★ (MRSA in community), Streptococcus pyogenes, Gram-negatives (diabetics)
Cloxacillin 500mg QID (MSSA). MRSA: co-trimoxazole + rifampicin (community). Diabetic foot: amoxicillin-clavulanate + metronidazole.
Manuka honey dressing (MRSA active ★★★). Tea tree oil 5–10% (antiseptic). Turmeric paste (anti-inflammatory). Refer urgently: spreading cellulitis, crepitus, systemic features.
Gastroenteritis (acute)
Viral: Norovirus ★, Rotavirus (children). Bacterial: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli (ETEC travellers), V. cholerae (epidemic)
Most viral/mild bacterial: supportive only (ORS). Severe/systemic: ciprofloxacin 500mg BD × 3d. Cholera: doxycycline single dose.

ORS (WHO formula: 1L water + 6 tsp sugar + ½ tsp salt). Zinc 20mg/day × 10 days ★★★ (reduces severity + duration in children). Saccharomyces boulardii 500mg BD. Probiotic post-infection. Avoid antidiarrhoeals in bloody diarrhoea (may worsen).
Tuberculosis (TB)
Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Drug-resistant TB (MDR/XDR) increasing in India.
DOTS: Isoniazid + Rifampicin + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol × 2 months; then INH + RIF × 4 months. Never naturopathic-only management.

Nutritional support critical: protein 1.5g/kg, zinc 30mg, selenium 200mcg, Vit D 4000IU (deficiency = ↑ TB reactivation risk ★★★). Pyridoxine 50mg/day to prevent INH neuropathy. Milk thistle (rifampicin hepatotoxicity protection — consult).
Dengue Fever
Dengue virus (DENV 1–4), Aedes aegypti mosquito
No specific antiviral. Supportive: paracetamol only (NO aspirin/ibuprofen — platelet dysfunction). IV fluids if severe. Monitor platelet count daily.

Carica papaya leaf extract ★★★ (RCT India: ↑ platelet count significantly in dengue thrombocytopenia). Vit C IV (antioxidant, antiviral). Giloy (Tinospora cordifolia) — immunomodulatory. Adequate hydration. NO NSAIDs.

cards.title: Antibiotic Guide — Empirical Treatment (India-Specific)

alerts.label: Antibiotic Stewardship — The BNYS Practitioner's Role
alerts.text: India has among the highest rates of antibiotic resistance globally. BNYS practitioners play a critical role in stewardship: (1) Never prescribe antibiotics for viral URTI. (2) Educate patients about course completion. (3) Identify when antibiotics are truly needed vs when naturopathic management suffices. (4) Recognise resistance patterns — local antibiograms differ from textbooks.

rows: Penicillins
Amoxicillin, ampicillin, cloxacillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate
Gram-positive ★, some Gram-negative (amox-clav)
GI upset, allergic reactions (1:10,000 anaphylaxis), C.diff (rare)
Probiotics 2hrs from dose. Allergy: always ask before prescribing. PCN allergy → cephalosporins (10% cross-reactivity — use with caution).
Fluoroquinolones
Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, ofloxacin
Broad-spectrum G+ and G-. Atypicals. UTI, respiratory, GI
Tendinopathy/rupture ★ (Achilles), QT prolongation, photosensitivity, C.diff, dysbiosis, peripheral neuropathy (rare but serious)
Avoid with Mg/Ca/Zn/Al (chelation — ↓ absorption). Photosensitivity: SPF 50. Tendon pain → STOP and refer. Gut: probiotics + glutamine post-course.
Macrolides
Azithromycin, clarithromycin, erythromycin
Atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella), S. pneumoniae, H. pylori (clarithromycin)
QT prolongation (azithromycin — ★ caution with other QT drugs), GI (erythromycin — worst GI), hepatotoxicity (clarithromycin)
CYP3A4 inhibitor (clarithromycin) — check drug interactions. Avoid with cardiac history + QT prolonging drugs.
Tetracyclines
Doxycycline, tetracycline, minocycline
Rickettsia ★, Chlamydia, Mycoplasma, Brucella, Lyme, acne, malaria prophylaxis
Photosensitivity ★★★ (doxycycline), oesophageal ulceration (take with full glass of water, remain upright 30 min), GI, teeth discolouration (children <8 — avoid)
Photosensitivity: SPF 50, long sleeves. Take with food (not dairy — chelation). Avoid antacids, iron, Ca within 2hrs (chelation → ↓ absorption).
Metronidazole
Metronidazole, tinidazole
Anaerobes ★★★, protozoa (Giardia, Trichomonas, Entamoeba), H. pylori (triple therapy)
Metallic taste, nausea, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged), DISULFIRAM REACTION with alcohol (avoid alcohol strictly during and 48hrs after)
Probiotics (essential — profound dysbiosis). B vitamins (neuropathy prevention). Alcohol education — critical safety point.

cards._legacyHtml:
Antibiotic Stewardship — The BNYS Practitioner's Role
India has among the highest rates of antibiotic resistance globally. BNYS practitioners play a critical role in stewardship: (1) Never prescribe antibiotics for viral URTI. (2) Educate patients about course completion. (3) Identify when antibiotics are truly needed vs when naturopathic management suffices. (4) Recognise resistance patterns — local antibiograms differ from textbooks.

Allergy: always ask before prescribing. PCN allergy → cephalosporins (10% cross-reactivity — use with caution).
FluoroquinolonesCiprofloxacin, levofloxacin, moxifloxacin, ofloxacinBroad-spectrum G+ and G-. Atypicals. UTI, respiratory, GITendinopathy/rupture ★ (Achilles), QT prolongation, photosensitivity, C.diff, dysbiosis, peripheral neuropathy (rare but serious)Avoid with Mg/Ca/Zn/Al (chelation — ↓ absorption). Photosensitivity: SPF 50. Tendon pain → STOP and refer. Gut: probiotics + glutamine post-course.

Avoid with cardiac history + QT prolonging drugs.

Take with food (not dairy — chelation). Avoid antacids, iron, Ca within 2hrs (chelation → ↓ absorption).
MetronidazoleMetronidazole, tinidazoleAnaerobes ★★★, protozoa (Giardia, Trichomonas, Entamoeba), H. pylori (triple therapy)Metallic taste, nausea, peripheral neuropathy (prolonged), DISULFIRAM REACTION with alcohol (avoid alcohol strictly during and 48hrs after)Probiotics (essential — profound dysbiosis). B vitamins (neuropathy prevention). Alcohol education — critical safety point.

cards.title: Culture Reports — How to Interpret

pearls.label: 🟡 MRSA in India — Growing Concern
pearls.text: Community-acquired MRSA (CA-MRSA) is increasing in India — no longer confined to hospitals. Suspect MRSA when: skin infection fails to respond to cloxacillin within 48hrs, recurrent furuncles, household cluster of skin infections. Local antibiogram data essential — resistance patterns vary dramatically between cities. Treatment: co-trimoxazole DS (most CA-MRSA sensitive) + rifampicin for decolonisation. Hospitalised MRSA: glycopeptide (vancomycin/teicoplanin) or linezolid. BNYS role: manuka honey dressing is MRSA-active — legitimate adjunct for surface wounds.

steps.title: Reading a Urine Culture Report
steps.text: Colony count: ≥10⁵ CFU/mL = significant bacteriuria (symptomatic UTI). 10³–10⁵ = uncertain — repeat if symptomatic. <10³ = likely contamination. Organism identified: E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterococcus most common. Sensitivity panel: S = Sensitive (drug works), I = Intermediate (high dose may work), R = Resistant (drug fails). Choose S antibiotic with narrowest spectrum first (antibiotic stewardship).

steps.title: Blood Culture Report
steps.text: Two sets = 4 bottles. Positive: organism + sensitivity. Time to positivity: bacteraemia (faster) vs contamination (slower — >72hrs, coagulase-negative staph alone often = skin contaminant). True bacteraemia: same organism in multiple bottles, grows early. Common contaminants: coagulase-negative Staph (Staphylococcus epidermidis), Corynebacterium, Bacillus — significance depends on clinical context and source.

steps.title: Stool Culture — India-Specific
steps.text: Pathogens sought: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, V. cholerae, E. coli (ETEC, EHEC). Ova and parasites (O&P): Giardia ★ (most common), Entamoeba histolytica, Strongyloides, hookworm. H. pylori stool antigen (SAT): sensitivity 94%, specificity 97% — best non-invasive test for active infection and post-treatment confirmation. Sensitivity report: check before treating — increasing quinolone resistance in India.

1
Reading a Urine Culture Report Colony count: ≥10⁵ CFU/mL = significant bacteriuria (symptomatic UTI). 10³–10⁵ = uncertain — repeat if symptomatic. <10³ = likely contamination. Organism identified: E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterococcus most common. Sensitivity panel: S = Sensitive (drug works), I = Intermediate (high dose may work), R = Resistant (drug fails). Choose S antibiotic with narrowest spectrum first (antibiotic stewardship).

Time to positivity: bacteraemia (faster) vs contamination (slower — >72hrs, coagulase-negative staph alone often = skin contaminant). True bacteraemia: same organism in multiple bottles, grows early. Common contaminants: coagulase-negative Staph (Staphylococcus epidermidis), Corynebacterium, Bacillus — significance depends on clinical context and source.
3
Stool Culture — India-Specific Pathogens sought: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, V. cholerae, E. coli (ETEC, EHEC). Ova and parasites (O&P): Giardia ★ (most common), Entamoeba histolytica, Strongyloides, hookworm. H. pylori stool antigen (SAT): sensitivity 94%, specificity 97% — best non-invasive test for active infection and post-treatment confirmation. Sensitivity report: check before treating — increasing quinolone resistance in India.
🟡 MRSA in India — Growing Concern
Community-acquired MRSA (CA-MRSA) is increasing in India — no longer confined to hospitals. Suspect MRSA when: skin infection fails to respond to cloxacillin within 48hrs, recurrent furuncles, household cluster of skin infections. Local antibiogram data essential — resistance patterns vary dramatically between cities. Treatment: co-trimoxazole DS (most CA-MRSA sensitive) + rifampicin for decolonisation. Hospitalised MRSA: glycopeptide (vancomycin/teicoplanin) or linezolid. BNYS role: manuka honey dressing is MRSA-active — legitimate adjunct for surface wounds.
